授業目的: This course provides a comprehensive overview of economic history with a focus on the evolution of economic systems, demographic transitions, and the foundations of modern capitalism. While titled under Canadian Studies, the course adopts a comparative approach, utilizing case studies from Japan's economic history (the Post-War Miracle, the Bubble Economy) and the British Industrial Revolution to illustrate universal economic principles. Students will explore the works of key figures like Adam Smith and Thomas Malthus to understand the theoretical underpinnings of today's global economy.
到達目標: Describe how key institutions and incentives shaped economic outcomes over time, with examples from Canada and Japan.Interpret basic historical indicators (real wages, prices, output) to compare living standards across periods and countries.Explain how demographic change affects growth and inequality.Compare historical development paths using a Canadian case and a Japanese case.
授業方法: This course uses interactive lectures and guided discussion. Students work with short readings, simple historical indicators (for example, prices and real wages), and comparative case examples from Canada, Japan, and Britain. The Start-up session is delivered on-demand via an instructor video and a short online task.

第1回:
Session 1 (Startup Week, 《90 minutes》): What is Economic History? Institutions, incentives, and “the economic problem” (Professor Dean intro video + short online task) On-demand contents, schedule fixed

Introductory video:https://youtu.be/dygdTZmiGcA
Short Online Task: See attached file below
第2回: Session 2 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): How Economies Coordinate: reciprocity, redistribution, markets (tradition, command, market)
第3回: Session 3 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Pre-Modern Trade and States: mercantilism, chartered companies, early globalization
第4回: Session 4 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Population, Disease, and Living Standards: Malthusian pressures and the demographic transition
第5回: Session 5 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Property Rights and the Rule of Law: why institutions matter for growth
第6回: Session 6 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Capitalism and Firms: finance, contracts, and the rise of wage labour
第7回: Session 7 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Adam Smith in context: specialization, markets, and limits of “invisible hand” stories
第8回: Session 8 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Industrial Revolution I: energy, mechanization, and the factory system
第9回: Session 9 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Industrial Revolution II: productivity, urbanization, and inequality
第10回: Session 10 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Measuring Development: GDP, prices, real wages, and what the data misses
第11回: Session 11 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Japan’s Economic History (Part I): Tokugawa foundations and early modernization
第12回: Session 12 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Japan’s Economic History (Part II): Meiji industrialization and state-led development
第13回: Session 13 (Core Weeks, 《90 minutes》): Japan’s Economic History (Part III): postwar growth, the bubble era, and later challenges
第14回: Session 14 (Flex Hours, 《90 minutes》): Special topics and final project consultations
トピック.授業外学習2: Students are expected to complete assigned readings and prepare for mid-term and final project assessments.

教科書/Required texts0: No required textbook. Readings and supporting materials will be provided via the course site.
学生による授業評価の方法Course Evaluation by Student: Students complete the standard end-of-term course evaluation.
成績評価Grading0: 評価基準等　Grading Criteria etc.
